content:
# Disagreement Log — Numbers 20 (NUM_020)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 20:1 -- "stayed" vs. "dwelt" vs. "settled" (va-yeshev)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "dwelt" |
| LOGOS | "stayed" |
| PNEUMA | "settled" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "stayed" -- the most natural English term for a temporary wilderness encampment. The Hebrew yashav covers a range from temporary sojourn to permanent settlement; in the wilderness context, "stayed" best captures the transience.

### 20:3 -- "contended" vs. "quarreled" and "Would that we had perished" vs. "If only we had perished"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "contended ... And if only we had expired" |
| LOGOS | "contended ... Would that we had perished" |
| PNEUMA | "quarreled ... If only we had perished" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "contended" (EMET/LOGOS) -- preserving the connection to the root riv that names the place Meribah ("contention") in verse 13. "If only we had perished" -- the optative "if only" is more natural in modern English than "would that," while "perished" is preferred over "expired" as clearer in English without losing the gravity of the Hebrew gava.

### 20:4 -- "to die there, we and our livestock" vs. "for us and our livestock to die here"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to die there, we and our livestock" |
| LOGOS | "to die there, we and our livestock" |
| PNEUMA | "for us and our livestock to die here" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to die there -- we and our livestock" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew lamut sham anakhnu u-ve'irenu places the infinitive "to die" first with the appositional "we and our livestock" following, which the EMET/LOGOS rendering preserves.

### 20:5 -- "evil place" vs. "terrible place"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the evil place" |
| LOGOS | "this evil place" |
| PNEUMA | "this terrible place" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "this evil place" -- the Hebrew ra' means "evil, bad, wretched." "Evil" preserves the Hebrew register and the theological resonance of the term; "terrible" is too interpretive.

### 20:5 -- "seed" vs. "grain"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "seed" |
| LOGOS | "seed" |
| PNEUMA | "grain" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "seed" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew zera means "seed" and refers to sown crops generally, not grain specifically. "Seed" preserves the broader meaning and the literal Hebrew.

### 20:8 -- "it shall give its water" vs. "it will yield its water"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "it shall give its waters" |
| LOGOS | "it shall give its water" |
| PNEUMA | "it will yield its water" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "it shall give its water" (LOGOS). The Hebrew ve-natan meimav uses the simple verb natan ("to give"); "yield" (PNEUMA) is interpretive. The singular "water" follows standard English usage; EMET's plural "waters" reflects the Hebrew plural meimav but is unusual in English for this context.

### 20:10 -- "Hear now" vs. "Listen now"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Hear now" |
| LOGOS | "Hear now" |
| PNEUMA | "Listen now" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Hear now" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew shim'u-na is the imperative of shama ("to hear"). "Hear" is the more literal and formal rendering.

### 20:11 -- "twice" vs. "two times" and "came out abundantly" vs. "came gushing out abundantly"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "twice; and came out water abundant" |
| LOGOS | "twice. And water came out abundantly" |
| PNEUMA | "two times. And water came gushing out abundantly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "twice" (EMET/LOGOS) -- the standard English equivalent for pa'amayim. "And water came out abundantly" (LOGOS) -- the Hebrew mayim rabbim means "abundant/much water"; "gushing" (PNEUMA) is an interpretive addition not present in the Hebrew.

### 20:12 -- "believe in me" vs. "trust in me" and "to sanctify me" vs. "to treat me as holy"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me before the eyes of" |
| LOGOS | "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me in the eyes of" |
| PNEUMA | "you did not trust in me -- to treat me as holy before the eyes of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me before the eyes of" (EMET). The Hebrew he'emantem is the hiphil of 'aman, and "believe" is the traditional and etymologically precise rendering (the root of emunah, "faith"). "Trust" (PNEUMA) is semantically close but loses the biblical resonance. "To sanctify me" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "to treat me as holy" (PNEUMA), which is a paraphrase.

### 20:13 -- "contended" vs. "quarreled" and "he was sanctified" vs. "he showed himself holy"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" |
| LOGOS | "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" |
| PNEUMA | "quarreled with YHWH, and he showed himself holy among them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" (EMET/LOGOS). "Contended" preserves the riv root connection to "Meribah." The niphal va-yiqqadesh is best rendered as the passive "he was sanctified" rather than the reflexive "he showed himself holy," which is interpretive.

### 20:14 -- "Thus says" vs. "This is what ... says"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Thus says your brother Israel" |
| LOGOS | "Thus says your brother Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "This is what your brother Israel says" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Thus says your brother Israel" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew messenger formula koh amar is a fixed diplomatic and prophetic convention best rendered with the traditional "thus says."

### 20:15 -- "many days" vs. "a long time"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "days many" |
| LOGOS | "many days" |
| PNEUMA | "a long time" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "many days" (LOGOS). The Hebrew yamim rabbim literally means "many days." PNEUMA's "a long time" is a paraphrase that loses the specific idiom.

### 20:15 -- "dealt evil" vs. "did evil" vs. "treated us badly"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "dealt evil to us, Egypt, and to our fathers" |
| LOGOS | "the Egyptians did evil to us and to our fathers" |
| PNEUMA | "the Egyptians treated us badly -- us and our fathers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Egyptians dealt evil with us and with our fathers." SOPHIA adjusts the subject from "Egypt" (EMET) to "the Egyptians" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for English clarity, while preserving "dealt evil" for formal register over "did evil" or "treated badly."

### 20:17 -- "water of a well" vs. "water from a well" vs. "water from any well"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "water of a well" |
| LOGOS | "water of a well" |
| PNEUMA | "water from any well" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "water from a well" -- adjusting the literal "of" to "from" for English idiom, while rejecting PNEUMA's addition of "any," which is not in the Hebrew.

### 20:18 -- "lest I come out with the sword" vs. "or I will come out against you with the sword"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "lest with the sword I go out to meet you" |
| LOGOS | "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" |
| PNEUMA | "or I will come out against you with the sword" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" (LOGOS). The Hebrew pen introduces a purpose/fear clause best rendered "lest." PNEUMA's "or" is too casual for the formal diplomatic threat.

### 20:20 -- "heavy people" vs. "large fighting force"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "with a people heavy and with a hand strong" |
| LOGOS | "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" |
| PNEUMA | "with a large fighting force and a strong hand" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" (LOGOS). The Hebrew am kaved literally means "a heavy/numerous people." PNEUMA's "large fighting force" is interpretive and loses the important adjective kaved ("heavy"), which echoes Pharaoh's hardened heart and YHWH's strong hand in the Exodus narrative.

### 20:24 -- "my mouth" vs. "my command"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you rebelled against my mouth" |
| LOGOS | "you rebelled against my mouth" |
| PNEUMA | "you both rebelled against my command" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you rebelled against my mouth" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew meritem et-pi preserves the vivid idiom connecting speech (the divine "mouth") to the command Moses failed to obey -- speaking to the rock rather than striking it. "My command" (PNEUMA) is a valid gloss but loses this crucial wordplay.

### 20:26 -- "shall be gathered and shall die" vs. "will be gathered ... and will die"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shall be gathered and shall die" |
| LOGOS | "shall be gathered and shall die" |
| PNEUMA | "will be gathered ... and will die" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "shall be gathered and shall die" (EMET/LOGOS). The formal "shall" preserves the gravity of divine decree; "will" (PNEUMA) reduces the solemnity.

### 20:29 -- "perished" vs. "expired" vs. "died" (gava)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "had expired" |
| LOGOS | "had perished" |
| PNEUMA | "had died" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "had perished" (LOGOS). The Hebrew gava is a marked term meaning "to breathe one's last, expire, perish" -- distinct from the common mut ("to die"). "Perished" preserves the distinctiveness and its echo of the people's wish in verse 3, while being clearer in English than "expired." PNEUMA's "died" erases the distinction between gava and mut.

### 20:29 -- "wept for" vs. "mourned for"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "wept for Aaron" |
| LOGOS | "wept for Aaron" |
| PNEUMA | "mourned for Aaron" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "wept for Aaron" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew va-yivku is specifically "they wept" from the root bakah ("to weep, cry"), not a general term for mourning. "Wept" preserves the visceral, physical image of communal grief.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
